To the Honourable, the Commons House of Parliament. The Information of Iohn Bargraue Esquire, shewing the seuerall abuses of the Gouernment of the Plantation in Virginia against Sir Thomas Smith knight.Alderman Iohnson and alias. SHewing to this Honourable House as I haue formerly done in the last Parliament (where it was receiued as a greiuance, Amongst many Articles that punish offences with death, the 15. being death for trading with the Indians, the 35. death for carrying a Barke or Pinnace out of the Riuer, the 36 death for selling, or giuing any Commoditie to bee transported out of the Collonie; are those by which the Petititioner suffered dammage.And because it was iudged that the Planter would be too much grieued with these Lawes, they haue made it death, Article 12. to vtter vnseemely speeches either against his Maiestie or the Comittees of the Virginia Company, or against any booke that shall be set out and published, intimating thereby this booke to bee published by the authoritie of the Company. as by an Order of the said Parliament appeareth) That whereas the Kings Maies

tie for the aduancement of the Plantation, did by his Patent encorporate a Free Companie, the Bodie whereof consisting of a Treasurer, or his Deputie, foure Councellors, and fifteene Commoners; who being tied to make their Lawes (aswell by the said Patent as by certaine Royall instructions giuen by his Maiestie) according to the Lawes of England, both they themselues and such as were admitted by them to be of the Companie, were to haue free Trade in Virginia, as by the said Parent appeareth. Your Petitioner seauen yeares since being encouraged to aduenture in the said Company by a Patent that promised him free Trade, he found himselfe, his estate, and those he employed, inthralled to the willes of a few, who abusing the publique, or any priuate Planter as they listed, they layd all the fault on the Company. 1. First, therefore your Petitioner accuseth Sir Thomas Smith and some others vnknowne, in the Companies name, and without the Companies consent (contrarie to his Maiesties Patent and Royall Instructions, and to the Common Lawes of England, haue caused to be printed and sent to Vi

rginia a certaine Booke of tirannicall Gouernment, whereby many of the Kings Subiects there lost their liues, and were brought into slauery, and the Petitioner and those he imployed were thereby much damnified, to their great losse. 2. That the said Sir Thomas Smith, Alderman Iohnson, and others (terming themselues the Company) haue contrary to the Patent and Instructions (by practise) made a Monopolie of the Plantation, and of the labours of all the Planters there, sinking or raysing the prises of Commodities as three or foure of them list, barring free trade from any other free of the Company but themselues, selling their Goods before their faces, and by setting of prises only of Tobacco and Sarsafras, they caused all other Staple Commodities to be neglected there; so as eight or ten Shippes going to Virginia in one yeare, all came from thence emptie, except the Megazine Shippe. 3. That the said Sir Thomas Smith practising with three, foure, or fiue, which he tearmeth the Company, by secret instructions giuen to the Gouernours in Virginia, to barre the Petitioners Trade by tyrannou

s Lawes there, which made it death to Trade, by detayning of the Petitioners Goods by fraudulent sale of them, and by making stay of his Shippes, haue preiudiced your Petitioner in Moneys and vse money to the value of ten thousand pounds: Which abuses your Petitioner hath made proofe of them in Chancerie vpon a full hearing there before the Right Honourable the Lord Keeper, who because he could not distinguish the Actions of the Defendants from the Actions of the Company, he found it to be matter of State, and referred your Petitioner to the Councell-Table. 4. That Sir Thomas Smiths Accompts for the moneys receiued for Virginia are false, grosse and vnexaminable, because the originalls being lost, there appeareth neyther Warrants for the issuing of the Monies, nor Receipts for the paying of them.It were hard if Sir Thomas Smith should be censured by his owne Lawe, Article the 17. Death for making of a falle accompt. 5. That during the time that your Petitioners Cause and the busines of Virginia hath depended at the Board, Sir Thomas Smith (to conceale the iniquitie of his Gouernment

from his Maiestie) he hath caused the King to be misinformed concerning the Lord Keepers Decree, caused a mistaken Order (that cleane altered the sence of the Lords of the Councells Reports) to be deliuered to his Maiestie, caused a Commission for the rectifying of the abuses, and the settleing of a Gouernment for Virginia to sleepe and be of no force, (when there were neere vpon 80. Articles put in against them, and but foure of them examined) by vnderhand, offering a great some of Mony, whereby he hath thrust the blame of misgouernment on others, robbed the Common-wealth of her great Examples, and your Petitioner from meanes of recouering his Right, except he be releiued by this Honourable House. Your Petitioners Sure, is, that Sir Thomas Smith may forthwith be required to make answere to these Articles, and that the abuses of encroaching on Soueraigne power, of abusing publique Authoritie to priuate ends, of robbing the publique Treasurie, being examined and prooued, your Petitiouer may find reliefe for his particuler wrongs, and the Commonwealth may not loose her life of Examples

; And your Petitioner shall be bound to pray for you, etc. The words of the Kings Instructions are, viz. And the offences of Tumults, Rebellions, Conspiracie, Mutinie, and Seditions in those parts, which be dangerous to the State there; together with Murther Manslaughter, Incest, Rapes, and Adulteries and no other offences shalbe punished by death. Whereas Sir Thomas Smith hath dispersed amongst this Honourable Assembly, diuers Papers mixed with many falshoods, among some truthes, endeauouring thereby to auoid this Honourable Triall, (as he hath formerly done all other courses of Iustice) though this alone doth shew his guiltinesse: yet the proouing of the 5. Article, wherein his abusing of the King, being the very Fountaine of Iustice, will not onely appeare, but it will also make the falsehood of his dispersed papers more euident, and tender him the more culpable for vsing such shifts.

by the lord deputy and council henry falkland whereas for prevention of such disorder riot and rebellion within this realm as may grow by loose and disloyal people have mean to arm themselves to do mischief against his majesty and the common wealth diverse proclamation have be heretofore publish by the late Lo: deputy and council of this kingdom command that no master or owner of any ship bark or any merchant factor or other tradseman nor any other person or person whatsoever denizen or stranger shall bring or cause to be bring into this kingdom or any part thereof without special licence from the lord of the council in England or from the lord deputy and council here any quantity or quantity of gunpowder or any musket calliver hand-gunns pistol or other piece morrion head-piece armour or any other furniture for the war sword and rapier and sword and rapier blade only except upon pain of his majesty displeasure and indignation and upon p^ine of se^sure and so feature of the same whereof the one moiety to be to the use of the king most excellent majesty and the other moiety to the use

of such person or person as shall so discover and seize the same to his majesty use and we be now again inform that the say proclamation have of late be little regard but that great quantity of powder arm and other warlike munition of all kind have be daily bring into this kingdom from foreign part and sale thereof make to all sort of people without respect of person so as many loose ^earnes and idle person of bad and lewd behaviour be furnish and arm with powder piece and other warlike weapon do not only make great spoil and havoc of red and fallow deer and other game within the proper land of sundry nobleman and gentleman of this kingdom but be thereby the rather embolden to commit diverse insolent riot burglary robbery and murder and many time to run into open action of rebellion to the great disturbance of the public peace and trouble of the state and commonwealth of this kingdom by mean whereof great mischief and inconvenience may happen in this realm if due care and circumspection be not speedy have for prevent the same and although after so many warning give we may just call

such person to account as have offend against the say proclamation by impose the utmost penalty that by the tenor of the same may be inflict upon they for their wilful contempt against his majesty and the state in that behalf yet hope that by the renew of the say former proclamation once more the same will from henceforth be more strict observe and keep then heretofore it have be we therefore do again in the name of the king most excellent majesty declare publish and command that no master or owner of any ship or bark or any merchant factor or other tradesman nor any other person or person whatsoever denizen or stranger shall from henceforth bring or cause to be bring into this kingdom or any part thereof without special licence from the lord of the council in England or from we the lord deputy and council of this kingdom any quantity or quantity of gunpowder or any musket caliver handgun pistol or other piece morion head-piece armour or other furniture for the war sword and rapier and sword and rapier blade only except upon pain of his majesty displeasure and indignation and upon pa

in of seizure and forfeiture of the same whereof the one moiety shall be to the use of the king majesty and the one half of the other moiety to be for the officer of his majesty store where the same be seize and the other moiety and half of the aforesaid moiety to be give to he who shall discover and seize the same or the value thereof according to his majesty rate and we do further in his majesty name declare and publish that if any merchant factor or tradesman or any other person or person within this realm now have or at any time hereafter shall happen to have any quantity or quantity of gunpowder or any musket caliver handgun or other piece morion head-piece armour or other abiliment of war sword and rapier and sword and rapier blade except in his or their hand or possession to the intent to sell the same or any part thereof to any person or person that then such merchant factor or other person or person within one month after the date of this proclamation or within one month after the say powder or munition shall come to he or their hand shall upon pain of seizure and forfeiture

thereof and upon pain of his majesty displeasure and indignation bring or cause to be bring all such powder and other munition into one of his majesty store of ordinance and munition whereupon we the lord deputy and council shall from time to time take order and provide that the officer of his majesty ordinance and munition shall receive the same and the owner or owner thereof immediate upon the delivery thereof into his majesty store be full satisfy and pa^ed for the same at such rate and price as the master of the ordnance have usual issue the same provide always that it shall and may be lawful to and for every city or town corporate within this realm to retain and keep within their several store such powder or munition for the necessary defence of the say city and town corporate so as the say corporation do within forty day after the date hereof certify we the lord deputy and council what quantity or quantity of powder or other munition they and every of they have now in their store and so as they nor any of they do not issue the same nor any part thereof by way of sale and we do

further declare and publish that if any nobleman gentleman or person of honest and good behaviour or if any city or corporation or any owner or master of bark or ship go to sea shall have occasion to use any reasonable quantity of powder or any musket calliver or other furniture of war that every such person and person and every such city town or corporation shall be from time to time by the warrant of the lord deputy or by the warrant of the several precedent of the province of Munster and Connaght or of other commander or governor within their several command furnish and supply of any such competent quantity of powder or munition as he or they shall desire to use at such reasonable and easy rate as his majesty do usual issue the same to his army within this land and we do hereby express charge and command all and every governor and governor mayor sovereign portriffes bailiff constable and all customer searcher and councillor within all and every the port haven and creek of this realm and all other his majesty officer and minister to who it may appertain that they and every of they

do from time to time use and employ their best diligence and endeavour to discover and leise to his majesty use as aforesaid such quantity of powder musket calliver hand gun piece and other munition and furniture of war as shall be bring into this kingdom or any part thereof to be sell to any person or person or which any merchant factor or other person or person shall detain and keep in his hand or possession to the intent to sell the same contrary to the purport of this proclamation and that herein they fail not to perform their duty as they will answer the contrary at their peril provide always that it shall not be lawful for any person or person whatsoever wear any irish mantle or trowses to use carry or keep any musket Calliver hand-gun pistol or other piece or piece nor to have or use any gunpowder nor to wear carry or keep any murrion head piece sword rapier skeyne or any other arm whatsoever invasive or defensive upon pain to forfeit the same to his majesty and that it shall be lawful for every nobleman gentleman or other person of honest and good behaviour conform he or the

mselves unto the english fashion of apparel to seize and take away the same and the same so seize and take away to carry to the next justice of the peace to the intent the same may be deliver into his majesty next magazine or storehouse of the munition and armour in those part or to such person or person as shall have oversight or command of the same and whereas many time heretofore instruction have be give aswell to the lord precedent in the several province as to the justice of assize in their several circuit and session for abolish the use of irish apparel and the reduce of all man to use a civil and comely attire according to the law and statute of this kingdom yet although they have according to the say instruction endeavour to take away the barbarous custom of wear mantle trowses skeynes and such like uncivil and uncomely apparel we see notwithstanding the use of they rather increase then any way reform or abate even to this day to the great contempt of authority and the disgrace of this kingdom among other civil nation for reformation whereof we have think fit hereby once more

to give all man notice that as his majesty have often recommend the care of the redress of the say barbarous custom unto we and abolish the use of such unseemly apparel so now W^ intend to inflict the utmost penalty of law upon such as from and after the time hereby prescribe shall presume either to wear the say barbarous attire or to suffer any within their family or rule to wear and use the same contrary to the law and statute of this kingdom and do therefore hereby straight charge and command that from and after the first day of August next no man do wear any mantle trowses or long skeyne nor do suffer any to be wear by any within his family government or rule but by the time aforesaid do attire themselves their child servant and follower in such comely manner as the civil subject of this kingdom now use to do in default whereof they be to expect no favour but to be proceed against for their wilful and obstinate contempt in the severe manner that by the law and statute of this kingdom may be take against they and beside such other punishment to be inflict upon they as the law do

impose we hereby charge and command that no person which from and after the time aforesaid shall wear or use any irish apparel or weapon shall presume to come nor shall be admit to have access or audience to or at the council table or in any court of justice in this kingdom nor before any judge or magistrate upon any matter or cause of private complaint whatsoever and we do likewise hereby straight command all sheriff provost marshal and other officer that as they shall find any man that after the say day shall wear any such long skeyne to take the same and break they and such man as they shall find wear of mantle or trowses out of their own house to take the same from they and before their face to cut they into piece that they never be wear again of the erecution whereof we require the say sheriff and provost-marshals and other his majesty officer to be very careful as they will answer the contrary and their neglect or remissness therein provide always that it shall and may be lawful to or for any person or person to use or wear any mantle within he or their house notwithstanding an

a publication of gviana plantation to all faithful and well affect christian for as much as diverse of this nation be depart hence into foreign country as well for the propagate of God truth most likely as their own private benefit whereby the word of the lord may be fulfil in those heathen through God infinite goodness and mercy as in we christian former say rom. 9 25. 26. ndertake by the right honourable the earl of Berkshire knight of the most noble order of the garter and company for that famous river of the amazon in America as by that which follow may appear but before I proceed further herein I think it not amiss to speak somewhat of the lawfulness of plantation in foreign country whereby such as make doubt thereof may be beet satisfy herewith now in plantation there be these two principal to be consider first whether it be lawful to remove from one place or country to another second whether it be lawful to possess a country already inhabit by other touch the former it have these two several head namely ordinary and extraordinary extraordinary be that when God shall please immediate to cause any man to depart from one country to another as he cause Abraham to depart from Haran to Canaan and jacob to return from Padan aram to the land of his father gen. 12. 1 4 5 31. 3. 18. but no man may now expect any such removal and call as these former have the latter of these which I call ordinary be that when man shall be either move themselves or by other

to leave their ancient habitation and go and inhabit some other place and this I take to be warrantable from that place of the king where the child of the prophet say unto elisha 2 King 6. 1 2 3 4. @ behold now the place where we dwell with thou be too narrow for we let we go now to jordan that we may take thence every man a beam and make we a place to dwell in and he answer go and one say vouchsafe I pray thou to go with thy servant and he say I will go so he go with they and when they come to jordan they cut down wood which work of they without question be no other to they then a new plantation for they see the inconvenience that be like to ensue by stay long in their ancient habitation do thereupon remove to a place more spacious and commodious for they @ in which progress of they we may learn not to conceal any thing to ourselves which may as well prove profittable to other but discover the same for their good as doubtless the child of the prophet here do from hence then we may gather th^ man may lawful remove with their family from one place to another and that for diverse cause

as here the child of the prophet do by reason of the straightness of the land wherein they dwell and it be to be fear that many also among we have as great cause to do the like by reason of the daily increase of all sort of people whereby city town and country be so throng that man can hardly live well one by another appear by the general complaint of many in these time neither have the say straightness alone 2 king 8. 1 2. cause a removal from one place or country to another ruth 4. 1. 2. but also famine 1 Chro. 10. 7. sword and other alteration happen in the world jer. 14. 18. and to speak true if that some in all age have not depart into other country with their family as God be please to move they by his divine providence the world assure have not be so much inhabit abroad as now it be Heb. 11. 37 38. and man have be more burden at home than now they be therefore such man deserve rather to be commend then condemn by any @ and that because they do nothing herein but what god have command to be do as appear gen. gen. 9 1. 1. 28. bring forth fruit and multiply and fill the earth an

d this again be ratify to Noah and his son say bring forth fruit and multiply and replenish the earth which place be no way contradict in all the scripture as I can ever perceive be sufficient to prove the lawfulness of plantation in all age and the rather when king shall be likewise please to allow the same freedom to their natural subject and that not only to the poor but also to the rich who have the like privilege of removal as appear by the place before mention gen. 13. 2 5 6 1^ and by that of Abraham and lot who depart one from the other because the land wherein they dwell be so straight that it can not bear they by reason their substance be so great so then we see all man have a like liberty of remove from one place to another except such only rom. 12. 7. which for the time be have any office or shall be marry 1 cor. 7. 5. in which case man ought not to depart from their wife without consent 1 pet. 3. 7. nor child from their parent without their like colo. 5. 20. 22. nor servant from their master without their leave it remain in the next place that I show the lawfulness of pos

e land be not the heathen as say the prophet Esa. 14. 1. lo I raise up the chaldaean to possess the dwell place @ that be not they Habak. 1. 6. neither be the heathen land the israelite gen. 24. 37. as God tell Abraham say know for a surety that thy seed shall be a stranger in a land @ that be not they gen. 15. 13. from whence we may gather that God permit not he to dispossess a people or nation of their ancient habitation for the enlarge of their own border nevertheless if such shall be willing that God people shall inhabit with they as Abimelech be willing that Abraham shall dwell with he in his land where it please he gen. 20. 15. and 47. 5. 6. and Pharaoh like well that jacob and his son shall dwell with he in the best of the land of Egypt in such a case man may doubtless possess a country already inhabit by other @ but the inhabitant of Guiana be willing that our nation shall inhabit that goodly and spacious country with they as appear more full in mr. harcourts relation of Guiana dedicate to the king most excellent majesty 1626. therefore without all question the plantation the

re make of late by our nation be lawful thus have brief show the lawfulness of plantation in foreign country for the better satisfy of such as make doubt thereof I come in the next place to speak somewhat of the nature of the river quality of the land climate and people of Guiana with the provision for man sustenance and commodity therein grow whereby such as be ignorant of the same may be encourage to adventure for the good of themselves and of those poor native which sit still in darkness and in the shadow of death as touch that great and famous river of the amazon in America Harcort it be call for the excellency thereof by josepus a the empress and queen of all flood and for the large extent of the same it be call by other the sweet sea suppose to be the great river not only of all India but also of the whole world it be say to slow from the mountain of Peru and run with many wind and turn for the space of 1500 league contain at the mouth thereof near sixty league in breadth in it be many land diverse of which be inhabit by the indian it do ebb and flow near three hundred league a

nd have in it great store of excellent fish for the use of man of which more shall be say hereafter when we shall come to speak of the provision of the country for man sustenance the quality of the land be of diverse kind as low middle and mountainous as here with we it abound in wood of diverse sort have plain in some part thereof with plenty of grass beside pleasant stream of fresh water all which be very needful and fit for a plantation the earth in those part be of sundry mixture as black mould clay and such like beside stone for build if occasion serve the season there differ much from we for the dry weather which we call there summer begin about August and the reins and wind which we account there winter begin about February which latter season be term by we their winter by reason of the rain then fall extraordinary and not by reason of the coldness of the weather for there be no frost or snow as with we here @ but a continual spring and summer season for no soon do the leaf wither and fall away but present it begin to spring again as here with we some year it seem the like for

when our autumn prove somewhat warm then common appear a second spring which yet never come to perfection and that by reason of the sun decline from our horizon and the cold winter present approach which cause always our latter spring to keep back until the time of our summer season @ which in Guiana be not so for there be little difference of heat and cold throughout the year but a most pleasant grow weather which common cause those part to be much more fruitful than many other part of the world @ the day and night be there always equal the sun rise at six of the clock in the morning and set at six in the evening or near thereabouts the year throughout the provision for diet be many as first the root call cassavie of which the guianian make their bread there be likewise a kind of great wheat call Maix it yield great increase and make excellent meal or flower for bread of the aforesaid Cassavie and this wheat be make a sort of drink call Paranaw much like the best march bear here with we other sort also they have which for brevity sake I omit here to speak of there be dear of divers

e sort wild swine hare and coney in great plenty beside which there be a great beast call Maipnry which in taste be like beef and will take salt there be also another creature which usual come into the fresh water and feed upon the grass and weed in the marsh and be call by we the sea-cow be in taste like beef and will also take salt as the former of these there be great store in their season they will serve well for the victual of ship homeward bind as of late have be prove of fowl there be diverse kind namely wild-duck wild-goose hen heron crane stork pheasant partridge pigeon stockdove parrot of sundry sort beside ravenous fowl and hawk with diverse other sort not know in these part the variety of fish be great as first mullet seabreame sole scate thornebacke swordfish seal a fish like a salmon but differ in colour shrimp lobster and oyster other sort there be beside freshwater fish of many kind which in these part of Europe be not know to we the fruit there grow be the Pina Plantana Medler plumb and nut of diverse kind beside store of potato as for the Pina it exceed all other fr

uit in those part be in taste much like ripe straberry with claret wine and sugar the Plantana taste like a old pippin the meddler exceed we in greatness and the nut in sweetness have thus brief touch the several sort of provision necessary for man sustenance it remain I show the variety of commodity therein grow for the trade of merchandise which in shorttime by god assist our endeavour may be bring to return great profit to the undertaker the first commodity of estimation there grow be sugarcane whereof in some part there be store which by orderly plant and erect of convenient work for boil and make of sugar may through God blessing be return yearly great profit to the adventurer @ as the plantation of sugarcane make heretofore in Barbary by the moor and since that in brasseile by the portugal may witness there be also cotton wool grow of the fine sort it be a profitable commodity for make of sustian calico and candle-wicke there be a natural hemp or flax of great use it be much like raw silk and be call of some silkegrasse there be likewise many commodity for dyer as namely a red

berry call Anotto which die a perfect oringe-tawny in silk and a yellow in cloth of which there may be get good store every season for myself with some other set forth a ship former for those part by way of trade receive upon return above three thousand weight of the say Anotto which then bear a good price here but much better in the neatherland other die there be beside hope of Cutcheneale which at this present I purpose brief to pass over there be likewise find of late store of nutmeg grow with their mace some of which I have recevy upon return of my foresay adventure which no man I suppose but myself can show the like except some gentleman which upon request have receive some of i which no doubt may in short time be bring to a more full perfection by either crop the old tree and dreaning the water from they or by transplant the young tree as by experience be common see how that nature be much help by art and industry there be much help by art and industry there be likewise Gumma Lemina Ginnipepper long pepper Cascia fistula tobacco spleen stone speckled wood honey and wax as for mineral or mine of metal it be very like

th which be the crown of their rejoice and who know but that he that infuse such desire into a inhabitant of the east indy may work as powerful in they of the west and may likewise bless these as he bless they with the ^lessed light of his most holy word to which let every faithful christian say amen now see that the hope of worldly gain cause most man to adventure in one kind or other and that the gain conceive of this plantation may prove very profitable both to the church of God and all adventurer it may therefore the better encourage and stir up man of all sort to become adventurer herein @ and chief such as have receive of the lord both grace and ability for the further set forth of his glory and although it can be deny but that our nation of late year have be very forward in settle of diverse plantation beyond the sea yet have they not all thereby so much glorify God as can be wish nor deal so faithful with some adventurer as be expect which thing no doubt be consider by many have be the only cause of keep they back from be adventurer and no marvel if when we consider the perso
